task: I’m putting together a simple inspection checklist for a used generator I might buy, and I want to make sure I’m not missing anything obvious. What order would you actually check things in, and which steps are worth documenting so you can tell later whether the seller really did the maintenance they claimed?

turns.agent_response:
Here’s a practical order I’d use for a used generator inspection, with the steps that are most worth documenting so you can verify claims later.

## 1) Start with identity and paperwork
**Check first:**
- Make, model, serial number
- Hour meter reading
- Fuel type and output rating
- Any service records, receipts, manuals
- Ownership/title if it’s a larger towable unit

**Document:**
- Photo of the serial plate
- Photo of hour meter
- Photos of all paperwork the seller provides
- Dates/mileage/hours on receipts

**Why this matters:**  
This is how you confirm the machine is what they say it is and whether the maintenance history matches the actual hour meter.

---

## 2) Do a full visual walkaround before starting it
**Check:**
- Frame, housing, panels, latches, rust, dents, cracks
- Signs of leaks under or around the unit
- Loose wires, missing covers, broken mounts
- Corrosion on terminals, receptacles, battery area
- Fuel tank condition and fuel lines
- Air filter housing, belts, hoses, exhaust parts

**Document:**
- Overall photos from all sides
- Close-ups of any rust, leaks, damage, corrosion, repairs, or missing parts

**Why this matters:**  
A lot of generator problems are visible before you ever turn the key.

---

## 3) Verify maintenance evidence before you rely on claims
**Check for:**
- Oil change records
- Filter replacement records
- Battery replacement record
- Coolant service record if liquid-cooled
- Spark plug service for small engines
- Load test or annual exercise records
- Generator head/alternator service if applicable

**Look for consistency:**
- Hours on receipts should roughly match the hour meter
- Service intervals should make sense for the machine
- Fresh-looking parts should align with recent paperwork

**Document:**
- Photos of receipts, invoices, and service tags
- Any date/hour annotations
- Photo of maintenance stickers or labels on the machine

**Why this matters:**  
This is the best way to tell whether the seller actually maintained it or just says they did.

---

## 4) Inspect consumables and fluids
**Check:**
- Engine oil level and condition
- Coolant level and condition, if applicable
- Fuel condition and smell
- Air filter condition
- Oil filter condition if visible
- Signs of fuel or oil contamination

**Document:**
- Photos of dipstick readings
- Photos of coolant reservoir/radiator cap area if accessible
- Photo of the air filter if you open it

**Why this matters:**  
Dirty, neglected fluids can reveal poor maintenance even if the exterior looks good.

---

## 5) Check the battery and starting system
**Check:**
- Battery age and condition
- Terminals for corrosion
- Cables for damage or loose connections
- Starter behavior
- Charging system output if you can test it

**Document:**
- Battery label/date code
- Photos of terminals and cables
- Video of start-up if possible

**Why this matters:**  
A weak battery can hide other issues, but it also tells you about general care.

---

## 6) Start it cold, if possible
**Best practice:**  
Ask the seller not to warm it up before you arrive.

**Check:**
- How easily it starts
- Smoke on startup
- Unusual noises
- Hunting, surging, rough idle
- Oil pressure or warning lights
- Fault codes or alarms

**Document:**
- Short video of cold start
- Photo/video of the control panel showing indicators
- Any fault codes displayed

**Why this matters:**  
Cold start behavior often reveals problems that disappear once the engine is warm.

---

## 7) Let it run and watch it closely
**Check:**
- Stable idle
- Smooth running under no load
- Exhaust smoke after startup
- Fuel leaks, oil leaks, coolant leaks
- Vibration or rattling
- Temperature behavior
- Fan operation and airflow

**Document:**
- Video of it running for a few minutes
- Photos of any leaks that appear while running

**Why this matters:**  
Some issues only show up once the engine is hot and vibrating.

---

## 8) Test electrical output
**Check:**
- Voltage output
- Frequency/Hz if the meter shows it
- Receptacles and breakers
- Transfer switch compatibility if relevant
- Load response if you can safely apply a load

**Best:**  
Use a known load or have the seller demonstrate under load.

**Document:**
- Photo/video of meter readings
- Photo of load being applied
- Any breaker trips or unstable voltage

**Why this matters:**  
A generator can run fine but still have bad output.

---

## 9) Do a basic load test
**Check:**
- Whether voltage stays stable under load
- Whether engine bogs down
- Whether it overheats
- Whether output remains clean and consistent

**Document:**
- Video during load increase
- Meter readings before and during load
- Any warning lights or shutdowns

**Why this matters:**  
This is one of the most useful real-world checks.

---

## 10) Shut it down and inspect again
**Check:**
- New leaks after running
- Hot smells, burnt wiring smell
- Loose fasteners or vibration damage
- Oil/fuel seepage
- Shutdown behavior

**Document:**
- Photos of any fresh leaks or residue after shutdown

**Why this matters:**  
Some leaks only show up after heat and pressure build.

---

# What’s most worth documenting
If you only document a few things, make sure you get:

1. **Serial plate and hour meter**
2. **All receipts/service records**
3. **Cold start video**
4. **Running/load-test video**
5. **Photos of leaks, corrosion, and damage**
6. **Meter readings during operation**
7. **Any fault codes or warning lights**

Those items are the strongest evidence if you later need to compare the seller’s claims with the machine’s actual condition.

# Simple rule of thumb
If a seller claims:
- “Fresh oil change” → ask for receipt and inspect oil/filter condition
- “Recently serviced” → check dates and hour readings on records
- “Runs perfectly” → cold start + load test
- “No issues” → look for leaks, corrosion, fault codes, and output stability

If you want, I can turn this into a one-page printable checklist with checkboxes and a notes section.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty solid order. Not bad. But I’d tighten a few things so it’s actually useful in the field.

- Put **paperwork and identity first**, yeah.
- Then I’d do **static inspection before any start attempt**: oil, coolant, fuel, battery, corrosion, obvious leaks, missing covers, hacked wiring.
- **Cold start** next, and only if the unit has been sitting long enough to matter. If the seller already warmed it up, I’d note that as a problem by itself.
- Then **run under load**. No load-only demo, that’s basically theater.
- After that, **shutdown inspection** for fresh leaks, smell, heat damage, loose hardware.

What I’d add that you didn’t really emphasize:

1. **Control panel alarms / fault history**
   - Photograph the screen before and after start.
   - If it stores fault codes, write them down or video the menu.

2. **Transfer switch / ATS compatibility** if it’s standby equipment
   - People love pretending that part doesn’t matter until install day.

3. **Exhaust and crankcase blow-by signs**
   - Especially on older units. If it’s puffing badly, that’s not “normal wear.”

4. **Service interval proof versus hour meter drift**
   - I’d compare receipt dates, hour readings, and obvious wear. If the numbers don’t line up, assume the paperwork is cosmetic.

For documentation, the stuff I’d treat as non-optional:
- serial plate
- hour meter
- control panel on startup
- cold start video
- load-test video with meter readings visible
- receipts/invoices with dates and hours
- close-ups of oil leaks, corrosion, battery terminals, filter condition, and any repaired wiring

One thing: don’t let the seller run it for 20 minutes before you get there. That hides a lot. I’d rather see a slightly ugly cold start than a polished warm demo.

If you want, I can turn this into a tighter checklist in inspection order, like actual boxes you can print and carry.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, field-useful order I’d use, with the **documentation points that matter most**.

## Used generator inspection order

### 1) Identity + paperwork
Check:
- Make / model / serial number
- Hour meter
- Fuel type / output rating
- Service records, receipts, manuals
- Title/ownership if applicable

Document:
- Serial plate photo
- Hour meter photo
- All receipts/invoices
- Any maintenance tags/stickers

---

### 2) Static inspection before any start
Check:
- Oil level/condition
- Coolant level/condition if liquid-cooled
- Fuel level/condition
- Battery condition and date code
- Corrosion on terminals/receptacles
- Obvious leaks
- Missing covers, broken mounts, hacked wiring
- Belts, hoses, air filter housing, exhaust parts

Document:
- Photos of dipstick, coolant reservoir, battery, terminals
- Close-ups of leaks, corrosion, damaged wiring, missing parts
- Overall walkaround photos

---

### 3) Control panel / fault history
Check:
- Warning lights
- Alarm codes
- Stored fault history
- Meter readings / status display

Document:
- Photo of panel before start
- Photo/video of any fault codes or history menu
- Photo of panel after startup too

---

### 4) Cold start
Only do this if it’s genuinely cold.

Check:
- Starts easily
- Smoke on startup
- Rough idle / surging / hunting
- Unusual noises
- Warning lights or shutdowns

Document:
- Cold start video
- Panel visible in the video if possible
- Any smoke/noise/faults

**If the seller already warmed it up, note that.** That’s useful info by itself.

---

### 5) Run under load
Don’t accept a no-load-only demo if you can avoid it.

Check:
- Voltage and frequency stability
- Engine response under load
- Bogging / overheating
- Breaker trips
- Output quality
- ATS/transfer switch compatibility if standby unit

Document:
- Video of load being applied
- Meter readings visible
- Any alarms, trips, or unstable output

---

### 6) Exhaust / blow-by / running condition
Check:
- Excessive smoke
- Crankcase blow-by
- Exhaust smell
- Vibration
- Hot spots / burnt wiring smell

Document:
- Video or photos if smoke/blow-by is visible
- Notes on smell, vibration, or abnormal noise

---

### 7) Shutdown inspection
Check:
- Fresh leaks after running
- Heat damage
- Loose hardware
- Seepage around hoses, seals, fittings
- Shutdown behavior

Document:
- Photos of any fresh leaks or residue
- Photo/video of anything that changed after run

---

## What’s worth documenting no matter what
If you only capture a few things, make sure you get:

1. Serial plate  
2. Hour meter  
3. Control panel before/after start  
4. Cold start video  
5. Load-test video with meter readings  
6. Receipts/invoices with dates and hours  
7. Photos of leaks, corrosion, battery, filters, and repaired wiring  

---

## Quick reality check on seller claims
- **“Recently serviced”** → receipts + hour readings should line up
- **“Runs great”** → cold start + load test
- **“No issues”** → panel faults, leaks, corrosion, smoke, and output stability should all be clean
- **“Standby-ready”** → ATS/transfer setup must actually match your installation needs

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checkbox checklist** you can print and take with you.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the usable version.

This order makes sense:
- identity/paperwork
- static inspection
- panel/fault history
- true cold start
- load test
- exhaust/blow-by/running condition
- shutdown inspection

And the documentation list is the right one. Serial plate, hour meter, panel before/after, cold start video, load-test video with readings, receipts with dates/hours, close-ups of leaks/corrosion/battery/repaired wiring. That’s the stuff that actually lets you call bullshit later if the seller’s story doesn’t match the machine.

The only thing I’d maybe add is: if it’s been sitting long enough, note that too. Freshly warmed unit, battery on a charger, anything like that. Those little tricks matter. Otherwise, this is solid.
